section.title: The Twelve Sent Out
section.sectionRationale: The second of Matthew's five discourses is the Mission Discourse. Having felt compassion for the shepherdless crowds, Jesus now multiplies His ministry: He appoints twelve apostles, gives them His authority, and sends them to the lost sheep of Israel. His instructions move from the immediate mission to the long haul of persecution, fearless witness, and the costly, dividing allegiance He requires.
section.thesis: Jesus extends His own mission and authority through the twelve, sending them to proclaim the kingdom in radical dependence, and He prepares them for hatred and persecution with the promise of the Father's care and the Spirit's help, calling them to fearless confession and cross-bearing loyalty that treasures Him above life itself.
section.frameNote: Seven beats: the twelve named and empowered; sent to Israel with instructions; sheep among wolves facing persecution; the call to fear God alone under the Father's care; confessing or denying Christ; not peace but a sword and the cost of discipleship; and the reward of those who receive His messengers.

verses.text: And calling His twelve disciples to Him, Jesus gave them {{exousia|authority}} over unclean spirits, so that they could drive them out and heal every disease and sickness.
These are the names of the twelve {{apostolos|apostles}}: first Simon, called Peter, and his brother Andrew; James son of Zebedee, and his brother John;
Philip and Bartholomew; Thomas and Matthew the tax collector; James son of Alphaeus, and Thaddaeus;
Simon the Zealot, and Judas Iscariot, who betrayed Jesus.

units.label: Sent to the lost sheep
units.note: freely you have received, freely give

verses.text: These twelve Jesus sent out with the following instructions: “Do not go onto the road of the Gentiles or enter any town of the Samaritans.
Go rather to the lost sheep of Israel.
As you go, preach this message: ‘The kingdom of heaven is near.’
Heal the sick, raise the dead, cleanse the lepers, drive out demons. Freely you have received; freely give.
Do not take along any gold or silver or copper in your belts.
Take no bag for the road, or second tunic, or sandals, or staff; for the worker is worthy of his provisions.
Whatever town or village you enter, find out who is worthy there and stay at his house until you move on.
As you enter the home, greet its occupants.
If the home is worthy, let your peace rest on it, but if it is not, let your peace return to you.
And if anyone will not welcome you or heed your words, shake the dust off your feet when you leave that home or town.
Truly I tell you, it will be more bearable for Sodom and Gomorrah on the day of judgment than for that town.

units.label: Sheep among wolves
units.note: the Spirit of your Father will speak

verses.text: Behold, I am sending you out like sheep among wolves; therefore be as shrewd as snakes and as innocent as doves.
But beware of men, for they will hand you over to their councils and flog you in their synagogues.
On My account you will be brought before governors and kings as witnesses to them and to the Gentiles.
But when they hand you over, do not worry about how to respond or what to say. In that hour you will be given what to say.
For it will not be you speaking, but the Spirit of your Father speaking through you.
Brother will betray brother to death, and a father his child; children will rise against their parents and have them put to death.
You will be hated by everyone because of My name, but the one who perseveres to the end will be saved.
When they persecute you in one town, flee to the next. Truly I tell you, you will not reach all the towns of Israel before the Son of Man comes.
A disciple is not above his teacher, nor a servant above his master.
It is enough for a disciple to be like his teacher, and a servant like his master. If the head of the house has been called Beelzebul, how much more the members of his household!

units.label: Fear God alone
units.note: the very hairs of your head are numbered

verses.text: So do not be afraid of them. For there is nothing concealed that will not be disclosed, and nothing hidden that will not be made known.
What I tell you in the dark, speak in the daylight; what is whispered in your ear, proclaim from the housetops.
Do not be afraid of those who kill the body but cannot kill the {{psyche|soul}}. Instead, fear the One who can destroy both soul and body in hell.
Are not two sparrows sold for a penny? Yet not one of them will fall to the ground apart from the will of your Father.
And even the very hairs of your head are all numbered.
So do not be afraid; you are worth more than many sparrows.
Therefore everyone who {{homologeo|confesses}} Me before men, I will also confess him before My Father in heaven.
But whoever denies Me before men, I will also deny him before My Father in heaven.

units.label: Not peace but a sword
units.note: take up your cross and follow

verses.text: Do not assume that I have come to bring peace to the earth; I have not come to bring peace, but a sword.
For I have come to turn ‘a man against his father, a daughter against her mother, a daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law.
A man’s enemies will be the members of his own household.’
Anyone who loves his father or mother more than Me is not worthy of Me; anyone who loves his son or daughter more than Me is not worthy of Me;
and anyone who does not take up his cross and follow Me is not worthy of Me.
Whoever finds his life will lose it, and whoever loses his life for My sake will find it.
He who receives you receives Me, and he who receives Me receives the One who sent Me.
Whoever receives a prophet because he is a prophet will receive a prophet’s reward, and whoever receives a righteous man because he is a righteous man will receive a righteous man’s reward.
And if anyone gives even a cup of cold water to one of these little ones because he is My disciple, truly I tell you, he will never lose his reward.”

exousia.gloss: Authority, right, power. Jesus gives the twelve His own authority over unclean spirits and disease, so their ministry is an extension of His; they act not in their own power but as those sent and empowered by Him.

apostolos.gloss: Apostle, one sent with the sender's authority, an emissary or commissioned representative. The word appears here for the first time in Matthew, marking the twelve as Jesus' authorized envoys, sent to carry His message and mission.

psyche.gloss: Soul, life, the true self. Jesus contrasts the body, which men can kill, with the soul, which they cannot, urging His messengers to fear God rather than men and to lose their life for His sake in order to truly find it.

homologeo.gloss: To confess, acknowledge, openly own. To confess Christ before men is to publicly identify with Him whatever the cost; Jesus promises to acknowledge such a person before the Father, and warns that denial will be met with denial.

basic.context: Jesus appoints twelve apostles, gives them His authority over demons and disease, and sends them out. He tells them to go first to Israel with the message that the kingdom is near, to travel light in radical dependence, and to expect both welcome and rejection. Then He looks past this mission to the long future: persecution, family division, and hatred for His name, all met by the Father's care, the Spirit's help, and the promise of reward.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the discourse on two levels: the immediate mission of the twelve to Israel, and the wider, ongoing mission of the church that faces persecution. The instructions about no bag or staff belong to that first, urgent errand; the warnings about councils, governors, and Gentiles look further ahead. Throughout, the theme is fearless, costly witness under the Father's providence.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus gives the twelve His own authority and names them apostles, sent ones who carry His mission. He urges them to fear for the soul, the true self, more than the body, and to confess Him openly before men. To confess Christ publicly is to be owned by Him before the Father; to deny Him is to be denied.
basic.typology: The twelve apostles correspond to the twelve tribes, marking the reconstitution of God's people around Jesus. The messengers sent with Christ's authority continue the pattern of the prophets, hated and persecuted as they were. And the cross the disciples must take up points to the Master's own cross they are called to share.
basic.christ: Jesus is the sender who shares His authority and whose mission the apostles carry. He is worth more than father, mother, child, or life itself, a claim only God can make. He is present in His messengers, so that to receive them is to receive Him and the Father. And He is the Master whose sufferings His servants will share.
basic.god: The Father's providence rules the whole mission: not a sparrow falls apart from His will, and the hairs of our heads are numbered. He is the one to be feared above all who can kill the body, and the one who rewards even a cup of water given to a disciple. The Spirit of the Father speaks through persecuted witnesses.
basic.crossReferences: Micah 7:6 lies behind the family division Jesus foretells. Matthew 28 sends the disciples to all nations, completing what begins here with Israel. Acts 4 shows the apostles bearing witness before councils. And Luke 12 parallels the sayings about fearing God and confessing Christ.
basic.oneStory: The compassion that wept over the shepherdless crowds now becomes a mission. Jesus multiplies His ministry through the twelve, sending them first to Israel and then, in the church, to the nations, with a message of the kingdom and a warning of the cost. The King who gathers a people also sends them, promising His presence, the Father's care, and a reward that outlasts every loss.

context.literary: This is the second of Matthew's five discourses, closing with the formula when Jesus had finished in 11:1. It flows from the harvest saying of chapter 9 and names the workers Jesus sends. The discourse widens from the specific mission to Israel, verses 5 to 15, to the enduring realities of persecution and discipleship, verses 16 to 42, so that it addresses both the twelve and the later church.
context.historical: Traveling teachers and their dependence on hospitality were familiar; shaking the dust off the feet was what Jews did on leaving Gentile territory, a solemn sign of rejection. Synagogue floggings, appearances before Sanhedrin councils and Roman governors, and betrayal within families were real experiences of the early church. Beelzebul was a name for the prince of demons.
context.canonical: The twelve apostles evoke the twelve tribes, marking Jesus as gathering renewed Israel. The mission first to the lost sheep of Israel, then to the Gentiles, reflects the salvation-historical order Paul describes, to the Jew first and also to the Greek, Romans 1:16. The discourse anticipates the worldwide commission of Matthew 28:19 and the Book of Acts.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To commission the twelve as Jesus' authorized messengers, to instruct them for their mission to Israel, and to prepare all His servants for persecution and division with the assurance of the Father's providence, the Spirit's help, the necessity of fearless confession, and the promise of reward.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Some instructions, take no bag or staff, fit the specific mission to Israel and are not fixed rules for all time, as Jesus later adjusts them, Luke 22:35-36. The abiding commands are to proclaim the kingdom, to fear God rather than men, to confess Christ, to take up the cross, and to treasure Him above all, which bind every disciple.

debates.question: What does Jesus mean by I have not come to bring peace, but a sword?

views.label: The gospel inevitably divides
views.case: Allegiance to Christ splits even families, as Micah 7:6 foretold; Jesus is not endorsing violence but describing the division His claims provoke between those who follow Him and those who reject Him.

views.label: A call to costly, undivided loyalty
views.case: The saying leads directly into loving Christ above family and taking up the cross; the sword names the painful cost of putting Jesus first, which severs lesser ties when they compete with Him.

debates.bearing: Both agree Jesus brings not the false peace of compromise but a division born of supreme allegiance to Him, though His ultimate aim is true peace with God.

debates.question: What does verse 23 mean, that they will not finish the towns of Israel before the Son of Man comes?

views.label: A coming in judgment on that generation
views.case: Many read the coming of the Son of Man as His vindication and judgment, realized in events like the fall of Jerusalem in AD 70, so the mission to Israel continues until that decisive coming.

views.label: The urgency and continuance of the mission until the end
views.case: Others take it that the task of witnessing to Israel will not be complete before Christ's final coming, stressing the ongoing urgency of mission until He returns in glory.

debates.bearing: Both hold that Jesus assures His messengers their mission has a divinely set horizon and that the Son of Man will surely come; the persecuted are to persevere until then.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus gives the twelve His own exousia, authority, over demons and disease, and calls them apostolos, sent ones who bear His mission and speak with His backing. Facing persecution, they are to fear not those who kill the body but the One who can destroy the psyche, the soul, the true self that outlasts death, and to lose their life for His sake in order to find it. And they must homologeo, confess, openly own Christ before men, with the staggering promise that He will confess them before the Father, and the sobering warning that denial meets denial.

typology.fulfillment: Corresponding to the twelve tribes, they mark the gathering of renewed Israel around Jesus, Matthew 19:28.
The disciples continue the line of the prophets, hated and opposed, sharing the rejection their Master endured.
The disciples' call to bear the cross anticipates and shares the Master's own cross, the path to finding true life.

study.christ: The whole discourse turns on the towering claims of Christ. He shares His divine authority with the twelve and sends them as His own emissaries, so that to receive them is to receive Him, and to receive Him is to receive the Father. He asks to be loved more than father, mother, son, or daughter, and to be treasured above life itself, a demand no mere teacher could rightly make, for it belongs to God alone. He warns that His servants will suffer as He suffered, the household maligned like its head. And He promises to confess before the Father all who confess Him, and never to forget even a cup of cold water given in His name. He is the Lord worth losing everything to gain.
study.god: The Father's sovereign, tender care frames the call to fearless witness. He rules so completely that not a single sparrow falls to the ground apart from His will, and He knows the very number of the hairs on our heads; His messengers, worth more than many sparrows, are utterly safe in His hands even when men kill the body. He alone is to be feared, the One who holds soul and body. His Spirit will speak through His witnesses before rulers, and He will reward even the smallest kindness done to His people. The God of this discourse is both the awesome Judge and the attentive Father who numbers our hairs.

crossReferences.note: The family division Jesus quotes: a man's enemies are those of his own household.
The mission widened from Israel to all nations, completing what begins here.
The apostles bearing bold witness before the councils, as Jesus foretold.
Parallel sayings on fearing God rather than men and confessing Christ before others.

study.oneStory: The King's compassion for the harassed crowds becomes a sending. Jesus multiplies His ministry through the twelve, dispatching them first to the lost sheep of Israel and, beyond them, to the church that will carry the kingdom to the nations. He does not hide the cost: hatred, division, and the cross await His messengers. But neither does He hide the comfort: the Father numbers their hairs, the Spirit fills their mouths, and He Himself will own them before God. The mission that starts in a Galilean village runs on to the ends of the earth and the end of the age.

quiz.q: What did Jesus give the twelve, and where did He first send them?

options.text: His authority over demons and disease, and He sent them to the lost sheep of Israel.
options.note: Verses 1 and 6 give both the authority and the initial mission field.

options.text: His authority, and He sent them at once to all the Gentile nations.
options.note: The worldwide mission comes later; here they are sent first to Israel, not the Gentiles.

options.text: A new law to teach, and He sent them to Jerusalem.
options.note: He gave them authority and a kingdom message, and sent them to Israel's towns, not specifically to Jerusalem with a new law.

options.text: Money and provisions, and He sent them to the Samaritans.
options.note: He told them to take no money and not to enter Samaritan towns on this mission.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus tell the disciples not to fear those who persecute them?

options.text: Because they can kill the body but not the soul, and the Father who numbers their hairs cares for them.
options.note: Verses 28-31 ground fearlessness in fearing God and trusting the Father's care.

options.text: Because persecution will never actually reach them.
options.note: Jesus plainly warns persecution will come; the comfort is God's care, not immunity from suffering.

options.text: Because the authorities have no real power to harm them.
options.note: The authorities can kill the body; the point is that God guards the soul and rewards faithfulness.

options.text: Because they should fear no one and nothing at all.
options.note: Jesus tells them to fear God, the One who holds soul and body, even as they do not fear men.

quiz.q: What does Jesus promise about confessing Him before others?

options.text: He will confess before the Father those who confess Him, and deny those who deny Him.
options.note: Verses 32-33 state both the promise and the warning.

options.text: He will reward public confession with earthly success.
options.note: The promise is acknowledgment before the Father, not earthly success.

options.text: Confession is a private matter that need not be spoken.
options.note: Jesus specifically speaks of confessing Him before men, a public acknowledgment.

options.text: Everyone will be accepted regardless of whether they confess Him.
options.note: Jesus warns that denial before men meets denial before the Father.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean that He came not to bring peace but a sword?

options.text: Allegiance to Him inevitably divides, even families, as loyalty to Christ comes first.
options.note: Verses 34-37 describe the division His claims provoke and the priority He demands.

options.text: He calls His followers to take up literal weapons.
options.note: The sword is the division caused by the gospel, not a call to violence; this misreads the image.

options.text: He opposes peace of any kind between people.
options.note: He brings true peace with God; the division is the cost of undivided loyalty, not opposition to all peace.

options.text: He came only to start conflict for its own sake.
options.note: The division flows from supreme allegiance to Him, not conflict for its own sake.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say about those who receive His messengers?

options.text: To receive them is to receive Him and the One who sent Him, and even a cup of water is rewarded.
options.note: Verses 40-42 identify Jesus with His messengers and promise reward for kindness to them.

options.text: Only receiving prophets, not ordinary disciples, brings any reward.
options.note: Even a cup of water to a lowly disciple is rewarded, not only welcoming prophets.

options.text: Hospitality to the messengers earns nothing in particular.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus promises such kindness will never lose its reward.

options.text: The messengers must never accept anyone's hospitality.
options.note: Jesus assumes they will be received and stay in worthy homes.

questions: Jesus sent ordinary men, including a tax collector and a zealot, as His authorized messengers. How does it encourage you that God sends flawed people to carry His mission?
The disciples were to travel light, depending on God's provision. Where is God calling you to greater dependence on Him rather than on your own resources?
Jesus said to fear the One who holds the soul rather than those who can only harm the body. How would a right fear of God reorder the lesser fears that control you?
Jesus asks to be loved more than family and treasured above life itself. Is there a relationship or comfort you are tempted to put ahead of Him, and what would it look like to put Him first?
Whoever loses his life for Christ's sake will find it. Where is Jesus inviting you to lose something for His sake, trusting that you will gain far more in Him?

translationNotes.note: Cleanse the lepers refers to those afflicted with skin disease under Leviticus 13; the disciples are given Jesus' own healing authority to extend His ministry.
Take no staff on this specific mission; Jesus later adjusts such instructions for a different time, Luke 22:35-36, showing these were situational, not permanent rules.
The words about family division quote Micah 7:6, describing the household strife that allegiance to Christ can provoke.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 10:1-42. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; poem, line-break, and note markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
